exp_L1_00.label: Diagnostic Accuracy & Reliability
exp_L1_00.description: Concerns about whether AI tools actually help us make better, safer diagnoses—or if they introduce new errors or blind spots.

exp_L2_00_00.label: False Positives/Negatives
exp_L2_00_00.description: AI systems that cry wolf too often or miss real pathology entirely.

exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: Too many false alarms on benign findings
exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: The AI keeps flagging tiny lung nodules that we know are just scars or artifacts—makes me ignore it when something real shows up.

exp_leaf_00_00_01.label: Misses subtle but critical findings
exp_leaf_00_00_01.description: It’s great at spotting big tumors, but completely glosses over early signs of bowel ischemia or subtle pneumothoraces in trauma cases.

exp_leaf_00_00_02.label: Inconsistent performance across body regions
exp_leaf_00_00_02.description: Works like a charm on chest X-rays but falls apart on wrist fractures or pelvic MRIs—feels like it was trained only on the easy stuff.

exp_leaf_00_00_03.label: Can’t handle rare diseases
exp_leaf_00_00_03.description: If it’s not in the top 100 common conditions, forget it—the AI acts like it’s never seen sarcoidosis or lymphangitic carcinomatosis before.

exp_L2_00_01.label: Overreliance & Deskilling
exp_L2_00_01.description: Worry that leaning too much on AI will erode our own diagnostic instincts and skills over time.

exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: Residents stop looking beyond the AI highlight box
exp_leaf_00_01_00.description: I’ve caught trainees missing obvious free air because the AI didn’t flag it—they just accepted the negative result without scanning the whole image.

exp_leaf_00_01_01.label: Loss of pattern recognition intuition
exp_leaf_00_01_01.description: When you outsource the ‘first glance’ to AI, you stop building that gut sense for what looks ‘off’—and that’s how seasoned radiologists catch weird stuff.

exp_leaf_00_01_02.label: Harder to teach differential reasoning
exp_leaf_00_01_02.description: How do I explain why something might be TB vs. fungal infection if the AI just slaps a label on it without showing its work?

exp_L2_00_02.label: Uncertainty Communication
exp_L2_00_02.description: AI rarely tells us how unsure it is—and that’s dangerous when stakes are high.

exp_leaf_00_02_00.label: No confidence scores clinicians can trust
exp_leaf_00_02_00.description: It says ‘87% probability of pneumonia’ but gives the same number whether the image is pristine or motion-blurred garbage.

exp_leaf_00_02_01.label: Doesn’t flag low-quality inputs
exp_leaf_00_02_01.description: AI runs on a poorly positioned portable CXR and acts like it’s diagnostic—when any human would say ‘repeat this stat.’

exp_leaf_00_02_02.label: Can’t express ‘I’m confused’
exp_leaf_00_02_02.description: Real radiologists say ‘this is weird—I need more info’; AI just picks the closest match from its training set, even if it’s nonsense.

exp_L1_01.label: Workflow Integration & Efficiency
exp_L1_01.description: Whether AI actually saves time or just adds another step that slows us down or creates friction.

exp_L2_01_00.label: Alert Fatigue & Interruptions
exp_L2_01_00.description: Too many pop-ups, banners, or auto-segmentations that distract rather than assist.

exp_leaf_01_00_00.label: Constant pop-ups during dictation
exp_leaf_01_00_00.description: Just as I’m describing a complex finding, a bright red ‘AI DETECTED MASS’ banner covers half my screen—breaks my train of thought every time.

exp_leaf_01_00_01.label: Auto-contours that need manual correction
exp_leaf_01_00_01.description: The AI draws tumor boundaries that spill into adjacent organs—I spend more time fixing its sloppy segmentation than doing it myself.

exp_leaf_01_00_02.label: Prioritization conflicts with clinical urgency
exp_leaf_01_00_02.description: AI flags a stable chronic nodule as ‘high priority’ while ignoring an acute bowel obstruction because it wasn’t trained on emergency patterns.

exp_L2_01_01.label: System Compatibility & Usability
exp_L2_01_01.description: AI tools that don’t play nice with existing PACS, voice recognition, or reporting systems.

exp_leaf_01_01_00.label: Requires switching between windows or logins
exp_leaf_01_01_00.description: To see the AI result, I have to alt-tab out of my PACS into some clunky web portal—kills momentum and invites errors.

exp_leaf_01_01_01.label: No way to suppress or customize alerts
exp_leaf_01_01_01.description: I can’t tell the system ‘stop bugging me about renal cysts—I know they’re benign’—so I just disable the whole thing.

exp_leaf_01_01_02.label: Slow load times during peak hours
exp_leaf_01_01_02.description: When the ER is backed up, the AI plugin freezes my viewer for 10 seconds—unacceptable when someone’s coding.

exp_L2_01_02.label: Time Savings vs. Verification Burden
exp_L2_01_02.description: The hidden cost of having to double-check everything the AI suggests.

exp_leaf_01_02_00.label: Spending more time verifying than diagnosing
exp_leaf_01_02_00.description: For every minute saved by AI detection, I lose two checking if it’s real—especially with incidental findings.

exp_leaf_01_02_01.label: No clear handoff protocol for AI findings
exp_leaf_01_02_01.description: If AI spots something I missed, who documents it? Me? The ordering clinician? Legal gray zone that eats up time.

exp_L1_02.label: Legal, Ethical & Regulatory Risk
exp_L1_02.description: Liability, compliance, and ethical dilemmas introduced or amplified by AI use.

exp_L2_02_00.label: Liability Ambiguity
exp_L2_02_00.description: Who’s responsible when AI contributes to a misdiagnosis?

exp_leaf_02_00_00.label: Unclear medico-legal responsibility
exp_leaf_02_00_00.description: If I follow the AI and it’s wrong, am I liable? If I ignore it and it was right, am I still liable? Courts haven’t figured this out yet.

exp_leaf_02_00_01.label: Fear of being second-guessed by non-experts
exp_leaf_02_00_01.description: A plaintiff’s attorney will pull up the AI report and say ‘the computer saw it—why didn’t you?’ even if the AI was hallucinating.

exp_L2_02_01.label: Regulatory & Compliance Burden
exp_L2_02_01.description: Keeping up with evolving FDA, HIPAA, and institutional rules around AI deployment.

exp_leaf_02_01_00.label: Frequent revalidation requirements
exp_leaf_02_01_00.description: Every time the vendor updates their model, we have to retest it locally—which takes weeks of phantom studies and committee approvals.

exp_leaf_02_01_01.label: Unclear data usage consent for training
exp_leaf_02_01_01.description: Did our patients agree to have their scans used to improve this commercial AI? We don’t even know—and neither does legal.

exp_L2_02_02.label: Bias & Equity Concerns
exp_L2_02_02.description: AI that performs worse on underrepresented populations, potentially worsening disparities.

exp_leaf_02_02_00.label: Poor performance on non-Caucasian body habitus or anatomy
exp_leaf_02_02_00.description: The fracture detector misses breaks in denser bones common in Black patients because the training data was mostly from older white women.

exp_leaf_02_02_01.label: Language or terminology mismatches in reports
exp_leaf_02_02_01.description: AI-generated impressions use terms unfamiliar to our referring providers in rural clinics—creates confusion and delays care.

exp_L1_03.label: Data Quality & Model Transparency
exp_L1_03.description: Issues stemming from poor or opaque training data and black-box model behavior.

exp_L2_03_00.label: Training Data Limitations
exp_L2_03_00.description: AI models trained on narrow, non-representative datasets that don’t reflect real-world diversity.

exp_leaf_03_00_00.label: Trained only on ‘clean’ academic hospital images
exp_leaf_03_00_00.description: Performs terribly on noisy, low-dose, or portable scans from community hospitals—where most patients actually get imaged.

exp_leaf_03_00_01.label: Label noise from inconsistent ground truth
exp_leaf_03_00_01.description: If the original reads had inter-observer variability, the AI learns to mimic disagreement—not truth.

exp_leaf_03_00_02.label: Temporal dataset decay
exp_leaf_03_00_02.description: Models trained on pre-pandemic data don’t recognize new patterns like post-vax lymphadenopathy—makes them obsolete fast.

exp_L2_03_01.label: Explainability Deficits
exp_L2_03_01.description: Inability to understand why the AI made a certain call—critical for trust and learning.

exp_leaf_03_01_00.label: Heatmaps that highlight irrelevant areas
exp_leaf_03_01_00.description: The AI says ‘pneumonia’ but its heatmap lights up the clavicle—not the lung. Makes zero clinical sense.

exp_leaf_03_01_01.label: No access to feature weights or decision logic
exp_leaf_03_01_01.description: Vendors treat their models like secret sauce—I can’t see which imaging features drove the conclusion, so I can’t validate it.

exp_L2_03_02.label: Versioning & Reproducibility
exp_L2_03_02.description: AI behavior changes silently over time, making audits and consistency impossible.

exp_leaf_03_02_00.label: Silent model updates without notification
exp_leaf_03_02_00.description: One day the AI stops flagging pulmonary emboli—turns out the vendor pushed a ‘minor’ update that broke sensitivity.

exp_leaf_03_02_01.label: Can’t reproduce prior AI interpretations
exp_leaf_03_02_01.description: If a case gets re-reviewed months later, the AI gives a different answer—but we have no way to roll back to the original model version.

exp_L1_04.label: Team Dynamics & Institutional Adoption
exp_L1_04.description: How AI affects collaboration, training, and organizational culture in radiology departments.

exp_L2_04_00.label: Role Redefinition & Professional Identity
exp_L2_04_00.description: Uncertainty about how radiologists’ roles evolve when AI handles routine tasks.

exp_leaf_04_00_00.label: Fear of becoming ‘AI quality control technicians’
exp_leaf_04_00_00.description: Instead of interpreting complex cases, I’m spending my day clicking ‘accept’ or ‘reject’ on AI outputs—feels like deskilling in real time.

exp_leaf_04_00_01.label: Tension between subspecialists and generalists
exp_leaf_04_00_01.description: Neuroradiologists love their niche AI tools, but body imagers feel left out—creates silos and resentment in the department.

exp_L2_04_01.label: Training & Education Gaps
exp_L2_04_01.description: Lack of structured guidance on how to teach AI literacy to residents and staff.

exp_leaf_04_01_00.label: No curriculum for AI critique skills
exp_leaf_04_01_00.description: We teach residents anatomy and pathology—but not how to spot when an AI is confidently wrong or biased.

exp_leaf_04_01_01.label: Senior radiologists struggle with new interfaces
exp_leaf_04_01_01.description: My 60-year-old colleague avoids the AI tool altogether because it feels like learning a new language mid-career.

exp_L2_04_02.label: Economic & Reimbursement Pressures
exp_L2_04_02.description: Financial incentives (or lack thereof) shaping AI adoption decisions.

exp_leaf_04_02_00.label: No reimbursement for AI-assisted reads
exp_leaf_04_02_00.description: We invest in these tools, but CMS doesn’t pay extra for using them—so admin sees it as pure cost with no ROI.

exp_leaf_04_02_01.label: Vendor lock-in through proprietary ecosystems
exp_leaf_04_02_01.description: Once you buy into one AI platform, switching costs are huge—even if better tools emerge—because everything’s integrated tightly.

exp_leaf_04_02_02.label: Pressure to increase throughput using AI
exp_leaf_04_02_02.description: Admin says ‘now that you have AI, read 20% more studies’—but doesn’t account for the time spent managing false positives.

res_L1_00.label: Model Interpretability & Trust
res_L1_00.description: Understanding how domain experts assess, interpret, and build trust in AI-generated predictions from virtual cell models.

res_L2_00_00.label: Reliability Assessment
res_L2_00_00.description: How experts judge whether a virtual cell model is trustworthy or reliable.

res_leaf_00_00_00.label: Identify metrics used to evaluate model reliability
res_leaf_00_00_00.description: Determine which quantitative or qualitative metrics (e.g., accuracy, biological plausibility, reproducibility) experts rely on when judging if a virtual cell model like CellForge is reliable.

res_leaf_00_00_01.label: Understand role of domain knowledge in reliability judgments
res_leaf_00_00_01.description: Explore how experts integrate prior biological knowledge with model outputs to assess reliability beyond statistical metrics.

res_leaf_00_00_02.label: Assess impact of uncertainty communication
res_leaf_00_00_02.description: Investigate whether and how communicating prediction uncertainty (e.g., confidence intervals) affects expert trust in gene perturbation outcomes.

res_L2_00_01.label: Explainability Needs
res_L2_00_01.description: What kinds of explanations do experts need to understand AI predictions about gene perturbations?

res_leaf_00_01_00.label: Determine desired causal explanations
res_leaf_00_01_00.description: Identify whether experts seek mechanistic or causal explanations for why perturbing one gene affects others (e.g., signaling pathways, regulatory networks).

res_leaf_00_01_01.label: Map explanation types to scientific goals
res_leaf_00_01_01.description: Link specific explanation needs (e.g., 'Why does gene X affect trait Y?') to downstream tasks like drug discovery or hypothesis generation.

res_leaf_00_01_02.label: Evaluate sufficiency of current XAI methods
res_leaf_00_01_02.description: Assess whether existing explainable AI techniques (e.g., saliency maps, feature importance) meet the explanatory depth required by geneticists.

res_L1_01.label: User Workflow Integration
res_L1_01.description: How AI-assisted tools fit into or disrupt existing experimental and analytical workflows in genetic research.

res_L2_01_00.label: Current Experimental Practices
res_L2_01_00.description: Document manual workflows for genetic perturbation experiments to identify integration points for AI tools.

res_leaf_01_00_00.label: Characterize manual perturbation workflow steps
res_leaf_01_00_00.description: Elicit detailed descriptions of how experts currently design, execute, and interpret genetic perturbation experiments without AI assistance.

res_leaf_01_00_01.label: Identify bottlenecks suitable for AI augmentation
res_leaf_01_00_01.description: Pinpoint time-consuming, error-prone, or cognitively demanding steps in current workflows where AI could provide value.

res_leaf_01_00_02.label: Understand validation practices
res_leaf_01_00_02.description: Learn how experts verify hypotheses generated through manual experimentation to inform AI-assisted validation support.

res_L2_01_01.label: AI Tool Adoption Barriers
res_L2_01_01.description: Explore challenges that prevent or complicate the adoption of AI-based gene prediction models in practice.

res_leaf_01_01_00.label: Identify perceived risks of AI use
res_leaf_01_01_00.description: Uncover concerns about false leads, overreliance, or misinterpretation when using AI models for gene perturbation prediction.

res_leaf_01_01_01.label: Assess compatibility with existing tools
res_leaf_01_01_01.description: Determine whether integration with common bioinformatics platforms (e.g., Jupyter, GenePattern) is a prerequisite for adoption.

res_leaf_01_01_02.label: Understand learning curve expectations
res_leaf_01_01_02.description: Gauge how much training or onboarding experts expect before they can effectively use an AI-assisted virtual cell model.

res_L1_02.label: Knowledge Discovery & Scientific Utility
res_L1_02.description: How virtual cell models can support novel biological insights and hypothesis generation.

res_L2_02_00.label: Scientific Inquiry Goals
res_L2_02_00.description: What kinds of biological knowledge do experts aim to extract using virtual cell models?

res_leaf_02_00_00.label: Prioritize knowledge objectives for perturbation modeling
res_leaf_02_00_00.description: Rank expert interest in goals such as identifying key disease genes, understanding gene-gene interactions, or predicting phenotypic outcomes.

res_leaf_02_00_01.label: Assess need for multi-scale explanations
res_leaf_02_00_01.description: Determine whether experts require explanations that connect molecular changes (gene expression) to cellular traits or disease phenotypes.

res_leaf_02_00_02.label: Explore hypothesis generation workflows
res_leaf_02_00_02.description: Understand how experts transition from model outputs to testable biological hypotheses in wet-lab settings.

res_L2_02_01.label: Supporting Evidence Integration
res_L2_02_01.description: What external data sources help experts contextualize and validate AI predictions?

res_leaf_02_01_00.label: Identify trusted external knowledge sources
res_leaf_02_01_00.description: Determine which evidence types (e.g., literature, pathway databases, prior experiments) experts use to corroborate AI predictions.

res_leaf_02_01_01.label: Evaluate need for integrated literature support
res_leaf_02_01_01.description: Assess whether linking model predictions directly to relevant publications or databases would enhance interpretability and trust.

res_leaf_02_01_02.label: Understand role of negative results
res_leaf_02_01_02.description: Explore how experts interpret and use cases where AI predictions contradict known biology or experimental results.

res_L1_03.label: Expert-AI Collaboration Design
res_L1_03.description: How to design interfaces and interactions that support effective collaboration between geneticists and AI models.

res_L2_03_00.label: Interactive Explanation Preferences
res_L2_03_00.description: What interaction modalities best support expert understanding of AI predictions?

res_leaf_03_00_00.label: Compare static vs. interactive explanations
res_leaf_03_00_00.description: Determine whether experts prefer fixed explanations (e.g., heatmaps) or interactive ones (e.g., drill-down, what-if scenarios).

res_leaf_03_00_01.label: Assess value of perturbation simulation controls
res_leaf_03_00_01.description: Evaluate interest in tools that allow experts to simulate custom perturbations and observe predicted outcomes in real time.

res_leaf_03_00_02.label: Identify preferred explanation formats
res_leaf_03_00_02.description: Learn whether experts favor visual (networks, graphs), textual (narratives), or quantitative (tables, scores) explanation formats.

res_L2_03_01.label: Feedback and Model Refinement
res_L2_03_01.description: How experts might correct or refine AI models based on their domain knowledge.

res_leaf_03_01_00.label: Explore mechanisms for expert feedback
res_leaf_03_01_00.description: Investigate whether experts want to flag incorrect predictions or provide biological constraints to improve future model behavior.

res_leaf_03_01_01.label: Assess willingness to co-train models
res_leaf_03_01_01.description: Determine if experts are open to contributing labeled data or corrections to iteratively improve model performance.

aligned_path_expert: Too many false alarms on benign findings
aligned_path_researcher: Identify perceived risks of AI use

conflict.expert_branch: Too many false alarms on benign findings
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify perceived risks of AI use

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on reducing false alarms from benign findings—like harmless lung nodules—is crucial because it directly impacts clinical trust and prevents alert fatigue that can cause real abnormalities to be missed.

pivot.limitation: Relying solely on observed false positives without systematically understanding the underlying perceived risks may lead to reactive fixes that don’t address why clinicians lose trust or how AI behavior aligns with diagnostic reasoning.
pivot.research_goal: Identify perceived risks of AI use, including false leads, overreliance, and misinterpretation, to proactively design trustworthy AI interactions.
pivot.compelling_reason: By uncovering these perception gaps early, you gain a system that not only reduces nuisance alerts but also aligns with your diagnostic intuition—making AI a reliable partner rather than a source of noise.
pivot.coarse_scenario: On a typical morning, you review scans with an AI that no longer floods your screen with irrelevant flags. Instead, it highlights only high-concern findings with clear context, so you stay alert and confident—and when it does raise an alarm, you know it’s worth your full attention.

aligned_path_researcher: Understand role of domain knowledge in reliability judgments

conflict.expert_branch: Loss of pattern recognition intuition
conflict.researcher_branch: Understand role of domain knowledge in reliability judgments

probes.question: Is your concern about loss of intuition more tied to reduced ability to assess overall image coherence or to diminished confidence in local feature interpretation?
Which matters more when detecting unusual findings: how visually distinct the anomaly is from background tissue or how inconsistent it is with expected anatomical patterns?
Do you rely more on rule-of-thumb shortcuts derived from high-volume cases or on rare-case memory triggers when evaluating AI outputs?
Is your intuitive alarm more likely triggered by a single abnormal image or by subtle deviations across a series of follow-up scans?
When something feels 'off,' is it more often due to incorrect organ positioning or unexpected texture distribution within a region?
Does outsourcing initial reads to AI reduce your willingness to act on low-confidence visual cues: yes or no?
Has your speed in recognizing complex multi-feature patterns (e.g., tumor + edema + mass effect) decreased since relying on AI pre-reads?

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If an AI system always presented findings with perfect statistical confidence but occasionally missed spatially coherent anomalies (e.g., displaced structures), would your sense of 'Loss of pattern recognition intuition' increase?
If you were required to review 100% of AI-flagged cases but never saw unflagged images, would your ability to detect subtle temporal inconsistencies across patient scans degrade?

aligned_path_researcher: Identify perceived risks of AI use

conflict.expert_branch: Constant pop-ups during dictation
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify perceived risks of AI use

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on minimizing disruptive pop-ups during dictation is highly practical—it directly protects clinical workflow and ensures diagnostic reasoning isn’t derailed mid-thought.

pivot.limitation: Addressing only the timing or frequency of alerts may not resolve deeper concerns about whether those alerts are trustworthy or clinically meaningful in the first place.
pivot.research_goal: Identify perceived risks of AI use—such as false leads, overreliance, or misinterpretation—to ensure alerts reflect genuine clinical value rather than noise.
pivot.compelling_reason: By understanding and mitigating these perceived risks upfront, you gain more reliable, context-aware alerts that clinicians actually trust and act on—turning interruptions into informed decision support.
pivot.coarse_scenario: On a typical morning, you dictate findings without jarring interruptions because the system only surfaces high-confidence insights tailored to your case. You feel confident acting on them, knowing they’ve been shaped by real clinician concerns—not just algorithmic output.

aligned_path_researcher: Identify perceived risks of AI use

conflict.expert_branch: Unclear medico-legal responsibility
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify perceived risks of AI use

analogy.source_concept: Fear of being second-guessed by non-experts

structural_mapping.inputs: AI-generated diagnostic suggestion presented alongside clinician judgment
structural_mapping.logic: External parties (e.g., lawyers, juries) interpret AI output as authoritative truth regardless of its reliability or context
structural_mapping.outputs: Clinician faces scrutiny for deviating from AI—even when the AI was wrong or misleading

analogy.explanation: Just as clinicians fear being judged by non-experts who treat AI outputs as infallible, the researcher’s focus on 'perceived risks' captures how users anticipate such misinterpretations—especially false leads that could later be weaponized in legal or professional reviews, even if the AI was never intended as a definitive authority.

assistance_payload.scenario: A radiologist dismisses an AI flag for a lung nodule deemed low-confidence by the model. The researcher’s method catalogs this as a 'perceived risk' of overreliance or false alarm. But the expert worries not about perception—but that a malpractice suit will treat the ignored AI as missed evidence, regardless of the model’s actual validity.

aligned_path_expert: Poor performance on non-Caucasian body habitus or anatomy

conflict.expert_branch: Poor performance on non-Caucasian body habitus or anatomy
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify trusted external knowledge sources

analogy.source_concept: Language or terminology mismatches in reports

structural_mapping.inputs: AI output (diagnostic prediction or impression)
structural_mapping.logic: Translation or validation against a trusted reference standard to ensure alignment with user expectations
structural_mapping.outputs: Clinically interpretable, contextually appropriate communication

analogy.explanation: Just as unfamiliar terminology creates confusion because it doesn’t match the referring provider’s mental lexicon, AI predictions—especially on underrepresented groups—need validation against trusted external knowledge (e.g., literature on diverse anatomy) to ensure they’re not only accurate but also clinically meaningful and equitable.

assistance_payload.scenario: A Black patient presents with wrist pain; the AI misses a fracture due to training bias. The researcher’s method flags low confidence and pulls literature on bone density variations by ancestry to support re-evaluation. But if no such literature exists for that subgroup, the system still fails—highlighting that evidence integration can’t fix absent or skewed data.

aligned_path_expert: Trained only on ‘clean’ academic hospital images

conflict.expert_branch: Trained only on ‘clean’ academic hospital images
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify trusted external knowledge sources

analogy.source_concept: Temporal dataset decay

structural_mapping.inputs: Training data reflecting past clinical patterns (e.g., pre-pandemic imaging norms)
structural_mapping.logic: Model assumes static disease/imaging relationships, failing to adapt when real-world conditions shift
structural_mapping.outputs: Misclassifications due to outdated assumptions about what constitutes normal or abnormal

analogy.explanation: Just as temporal decay causes models to miss new imaging patterns because they’re trained on obsolete data, relying solely on internal training data—without integrating trusted external knowledge—leaves AI blind to contextual evidence that could correct for unrepresentative inputs like community-hospital scans.

assistance_payload.scenario: An AI trained only on high-quality academic chest X-rays misreads a portable ICU scan with motion artifact as pneumonia. The researcher’s method would flag this by cross-referencing radiology guidelines and prior similar cases in trusted databases. But if those sources also lack community-hospital examples, the expert sees no fix—while the researcher still trusts the knowledge integration pipeline.

conflict.expert_branch: Silent model updates without notification
conflict.researcher_branch: Explore mechanisms for expert feedback

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on silent model updates without notification is critically important—ensuring system stability, auditability, and consistent diagnostic performance directly protects patient safety and clinical trust.

pivot.limitation: Relying solely on version control and notifications may not prevent future performance drops if the system lacks mechanisms to learn from expert observations in real time.
pivot.research_goal: Explore mechanisms for expert feedback that allow clinicians to flag errors or provide biological constraints to guide model refinement.
pivot.compelling_reason: Integrating structured expert feedback not only catches regressions faster but also transforms clinicians from passive users into active collaborators who help harden the model against future failures—making updates more reliable and clinically aligned.
pivot.coarse_scenario: On a typical morning, a radiologist notices an unusual pattern in AI suggestions and quickly flags it through a seamless interface. Within days, the updated model reflects this insight, preventing similar oversights across the hospital network—without waiting for a vendor patch or retrospective audit.

aligned_path_expert: Fear of becoming ‘AI quality control technicians’
aligned_path_researcher: Prioritize knowledge objectives for perturbation modeling

conflict.expert_branch: Fear of becoming ‘AI quality control technicians’
conflict.researcher_branch: Prioritize knowledge objectives for perturbation modeling

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your concern about being reduced to an 'AI quality control technician' is deeply valid—maintaining professional identity and cognitive engagement in diagnosis is essential to delivering high-quality, thoughtful care.

pivot.limitation: Relying solely on AI outputs without shaping the underlying knowledge goals can lock clinicians into passive validation roles, reinforcing deskilling rather than reclaiming diagnostic agency.
pivot.research_goal: Prioritize knowledge objectives for perturbation modeling
pivot.compelling_reason: By actively guiding which disease mechanisms or gene interactions the AI learns to model, you ensure the system supports—not supplants—your clinical reasoning, transforming you from a validator into a co-architect of diagnostic intelligence.
pivot.coarse_scenario: On a typical day, you begin by reviewing cases where the AI highlights novel gene-phenotype patterns aligned with your clinical priorities. Instead of just approving or rejecting outputs, you refine hypotheses with the system, deepening your diagnostic insight while steering research toward questions that matter at the bedside.

aligned_path_expert: No curriculum for AI critique skills

conflict.expert_branch: No curriculum for AI critique skills
conflict.researcher_branch: Explore mechanisms for expert feedback

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on the absence of curriculum for teaching AI critique skills is critically important—equipping future clinicians to recognize when AI outputs are confidently wrong or biased is essential for safe, effective care.

pivot.limitation: Even with strong critique skills, clinicians may lack structured ways to act on their insights, leaving flawed AI behavior uncorrected and recurring across cases.
pivot.research_goal: Explore mechanisms for expert feedback that allow clinicians to flag errors or provide biological constraints to iteratively improve the AI model.
pivot.compelling_reason: By embedding feedback pathways into clinical workflows, your trainees won’t just spot errors—they’ll actively prevent them from harming future patients, turning critique into lasting system improvement.
pivot.coarse_scenario: A resident notices an AI misclassifies a rare tumor as benign. Using a simple in-tool feedback option, she flags the error and adds a note about histological features the model missed. Weeks later, during a similar case, the AI correctly identifies the malignancy—because her insight helped reshape its reasoning.

aligned_path_expert: Unclear data usage consent for training

conflict.expert_branch: Unclear data usage consent for training
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify trusted external knowledge sources

analogy.source_concept: Frequent revalidation requirements

structural_mapping.inputs: New AI model update from vendor (like new external knowledge source)
structural_mapping.logic: Local validation process to ensure clinical safety and regulatory compliance (like assessing trustworthiness of external evidence)
structural_mapping.outputs: Approved, locally verified model ready for clinical use (like validated, corroborated AI prediction)

analogy.explanation: Just as frequent revalidation ensures each AI update meets local standards before deployment, identifying trusted external knowledge sources ensures each AI prediction is grounded in vetted scientific evidence before clinical reliance.

assistance_payload.scenario: A radiologist uses an AI that flags a lung nodule as high-risk. The researcher’s method cross-references this with peer-reviewed studies and genomic databases to confirm plausibility. But if the AI was trained on scans patients never consented to share, the expert rejects its use regardless of external corroboration.

aligned_path_expert: Pressure to increase throughput using AI
aligned_path_researcher: Identify bottlenecks suitable for AI augmentation

conflict.expert_branch: Pressure to increase throughput using AI
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify bottlenecks suitable for AI augmentation

analogy.source_concept: No reimbursement for AI-assisted reads

structural_mapping.inputs: Investment in AI tools without additional payment from CMS
structural_mapping.logic: Admin treats AI as cost-neutral productivity booster despite hidden verification work
structural_mapping.outputs: Net workload increases or stays flat while expectations rise

analogy.explanation: Just as unreimbursed AI reads create a false efficiency gain by ignoring cognitive overhead, identifying bottlenecks for AI augmentation can overlook the verification burden that offsets time savings—both assume AI reduces net effort without accounting for new validation tasks.

assistance_payload.scenario: A radiologist uses AI to flag lung nodules, expecting faster reads. The researcher’s method identifies nodule detection as a bottleneck and deploys AI there. But when false positives surge, the expert sees throughput drop due to extra verification—exposing the researcher’s assumption that AI always lightens cognitive load.

aligned_path_researcher: Prioritize knowledge objectives for perturbation modeling

conflict.expert_branch: Prioritization conflicts with clinical urgency
conflict.researcher_branch: Prioritize knowledge objectives for perturbation modeling

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on ensuring AI systems correctly prioritize life-threatening conditions over stable findings is essential—misaligned alerts can delay critical care and erode clinician trust in diagnostic tools.

pivot.limitation: Relying solely on current AI models for triage may perpetuate blind spots if those models aren’t systematically trained to recognize the full spectrum of clinical urgency, especially rare or atypical emergencies.
pivot.research_goal: Prioritize knowledge objectives for perturbation modeling to uncover which clinical features most reliably signal true urgency across diverse patient presentations.
pivot.compelling_reason: This approach ensures future AI systems are grounded in a deeper understanding of what truly constitutes clinical urgency, reducing false alarms and missed emergencies—directly protecting your frontline workflow and patient outcomes.
pivot.coarse_scenario: On a typical morning, the AI now surfaces only the most clinically meaningful alerts, having learned from robust models of urgency that reflect real-world complexity. Clinicians move confidently through their caseload, knowing interruptions align with actual patient risk—not algorithmic noise.

aligned_path_researcher: Identify bottlenecks suitable for AI augmentation

conflict.expert_branch: Misses subtle but critical findings
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify bottlenecks suitable for AI augmentation

analogy.source_concept: Too many false alarms on benign findings

structural_mapping.inputs: Subtle imaging features that could be either noise or early pathology
structural_mapping.logic: Applying a low-threshold detection rule that prioritizes sensitivity over specificity
structural_mapping.outputs: High volume of alerts, most of which are clinically irrelevant

analogy.explanation: Just as excessive false alarms stem from over-alerting on ambiguous but common patterns, identifying bottlenecks for AI augmentation often targets steps where human reviewers face high cognitive load from sifting through numerous low-yield cues—both involve tuning signal-to-noise trade-offs in diagnostic workflows.

assistance_payload.scenario: During trauma triage, radiologists scan for subtle pneumothoraces amid rib fractures and artifacts. The researcher’s method would flag this step as a bottleneck ripe for AI support due to its time pressure and visual complexity. Yet the expert worries AI might miss the very subtle signs it’s meant to catch, worsening outcomes despite workflow efficiency.

aligned_path_researcher: Understand role of domain knowledge in reliability judgments

conflict.expert_branch: Inconsistent performance across body regions
conflict.researcher_branch: Understand role of domain knowledge in reliability judgments

analogy.source_concept: Misses subtle but critical findings

structural_mapping.inputs: High-resolution imaging data containing both obvious and nuanced pathologies
structural_mapping.logic: Prioritizing high-signal, common patterns while underweighting low-signal, context-dependent cues that require anatomical or physiological reasoning
structural_mapping.outputs: Confident predictions on clear cases but silent or incorrect on early or atypical disease manifestations

analogy.explanation: Just as the AI overlooks subtle pneumothoraces because it lacks integration of trauma context and lung biomechanics, the researcher’s focus on domain knowledge examines how clinicians use that same contextual understanding to judge when the model’s output is trustworthy—even when statistical confidence appears high.

assistance_payload.scenario: When reviewing a trauma CT with a tiny pneumothorax near the apex, the expert recalls prior cases where similar findings were missed by AI. The researcher’s approach would map how the expert uses knowledge of pleural anatomy and injury mechanics to override the model’s low-confidence output. But if the AI confidently mislabels a skin fold as a fracture in an unfamiliar body region, the expert distrusts it entirely while the researcher still seeks to formalize the knowledge gap causing that failure.

aligned_path_researcher: Prioritize knowledge objectives for perturbation modeling

conflict.expert_branch: Can’t handle rare diseases
conflict.researcher_branch: Prioritize knowledge objectives for perturbation modeling

analogy.source_concept: Misses subtle but critical findings

structural_mapping.inputs: Subtle imaging signs that are clinically significant but easily overlooked
structural_mapping.logic: Pattern recognition tuned to dominant, high-frequency features while filtering out low-signal anomalies
structural_mapping.outputs: Failure to flag early or atypical disease manifestations

analogy.explanation: Just as the AI misses subtle ischemia because it prioritizes obvious tumors, perturbation modeling that ranks knowledge objectives by commonality will overlook rare-disease mechanisms—both systems deprioritize signals that fall outside high-frequency training patterns.

assistance_payload.scenario: A radiologist spots faint reticular opacities suggestive of lymphangitic carcinomatosis on a chest CT. The researcher’s model ranks gene-interaction discovery for common lung cancers far above rare metastatic patterns, so it doesn’t generate perturbations for this presentation. The expert expects diagnostic support for any visible anomaly; the researcher optimizes only for frequent, pre-prioritized biological questions.

aligned_path_expert: Residents stop looking beyond the AI highlight box

conflict.expert_branch: Residents stop looking beyond the AI highlight box
conflict.researcher_branch: Explore mechanisms for expert feedback

analogy.source_concept: Harder to teach differential reasoning

structural_mapping.inputs: An ambiguous finding that could map to multiple diagnoses (e.g., cavitary lung lesion)
structural_mapping.logic: Weighing competing etiologies by integrating clinical context, imaging features, and prior experience—rather than accepting a single label
structural_mapping.outputs: A reasoned diagnostic hypothesis with explicit uncertainty and alternatives

analogy.explanation: Just as teaching differential reasoning requires unpacking why an AI’s single-label output obscures alternative diagnoses, designing feedback mechanisms lets experts inject those same nuanced distinctions back into the model—turning passive acceptance into active refinement of diagnostic logic.

assistance_payload.scenario: A resident sees a lung nodule flagged benign by AI and stops evaluating for lymphadenopathy. The researcher’s feedback system logs the radiologist’s override noting sarcoidosis features. But when the AI misses free air entirely—providing no highlight to correct—the expert sees danger while the researcher’s mechanism stays silent.

aligned_path_researcher: Evaluate sufficiency of current XAI methods

conflict.expert_branch: Harder to teach differential reasoning
conflict.researcher_branch: Evaluate sufficiency of current XAI methods

analogy.source_concept: Residents stop looking beyond the AI highlight box

structural_mapping.inputs: An imaging case with both AI-highlighted and non-highlighted findings
structural_mapping.logic: Trainees anchor on the AI’s output and neglect independent assessment of the full data
structural_mapping.outputs: Missed diagnoses outside the AI’s attention scope

analogy.explanation: Evaluating XAI sufficiency is like checking whether the AI’s ‘highlight box’ gives enough context for trainees to reason beyond it—just as insufficient explanations prevent learners from reconstructing the differential logic needed to spot what the AI ignored.

assistance_payload.scenario: A resident reviews a chest X-ray where AI highlights a nodule but ignores subtle cavitation. The researcher’s XAI method shows saliency on the nodule but offers no comparison to TB or fungal patterns. The expert sees this as failing to teach differential reasoning, while the researcher may still deem the explanation sufficient for localization.

aligned_path_expert: No confidence scores clinicians can trust
aligned_path_researcher: Identify metrics used to evaluate model reliability

conflict.expert_branch: No confidence scores clinicians can trust
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify metrics used to evaluate model reliability

analogy.source_concept: Can’t express ‘I’m confused’

structural_mapping.inputs: Ambiguous or out-of-distribution clinical data (e.g., atypical imaging findings)
structural_mapping.logic: Model forced to assign a diagnosis without signaling uncertainty, just as it lacks a mechanism to say ‘I’m confused’
structural_mapping.outputs: Overconfident prediction with misleading confidence score, mirroring how AI picks the closest match even when inappropriate

analogy.explanation: Just as clinicians expect AI to admit confusion when faced with ambiguous cases, reliability metrics should reflect not just performance on clean data but also degradation behavior on uncertain inputs—yet current metrics often ignore this, producing false assurance akin to an AI that never says ‘I don’t know.’

assistance_payload.scenario: A portable chest X-ray is severely motion-blurred, yet the AI outputs ‘87% pneumonia’ without flagging poor image quality. The researcher’s reliability metrics might still rate the model highly based on average accuracy across clean datasets. The models diverge when the expert demands context-aware uncertainty signals while the researcher relies on aggregate metrics blind to input fidelity.

conflict.expert_branch: Doesn’t flag low-quality inputs
conflict.researcher_branch: Explore mechanisms for expert feedback

analogy.source_concept: Can’t express ‘I’m confused’

structural_mapping.inputs: Ambiguous or low-quality imaging data that lacks diagnostic clarity
structural_mapping.logic: System forced to produce a best-guess diagnosis without signaling uncertainty or data inadequacy
structural_mapping.outputs: Confident-seeming prediction with no indication that the input was uninterpretable

analogy.explanation: Just as an AI that can’t say ‘I’m confused’ masks its inability to interpret poor inputs, mechanisms for expert feedback must first recognize when the model shouldn’t have spoken at all—so experts can teach it to withhold judgment on junk data, not just correct wrong answers.

assistance_payload.scenario: A portable CXR is taken with severe rotation and overexposure, clearly non-diagnostic to any radiologist. The researcher’s feedback system logs the clinician’s correction of the AI’s pneumonia call as training data. But the expert insists the real failure was the AI speaking at all—feedback should trigger input rejection, not label refinement.

aligned_path_researcher: Identify bottlenecks suitable for AI augmentation

conflict.expert_branch: Can’t express ‘I’m confused’
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify bottlenecks suitable for AI augmentation

probes.question: When encountering an ambiguous image, do you prioritize proceeding with your best guess or explicitly flagging the need for more data?
Is your expression of confusion more influenced by the clinical context or by the image quality itself?
Which matters more when you feel uncertain: acknowledging the limits of your knowledge or maintaining diagnostic momentum?
Do you require a single atypical feature or multiple inconsistent findings before labeling a case as 'weird'?
Is your internal confidence level more tied to your years of experience or to the presence of textbook imaging features?
Which factor more strongly determines if you have 'enough info': availability of prior scans or clarity of current anatomy?
When uncertain, do you more often consult a colleague immediately or first attempt additional image reconstructions?
Does your judgment rely more on similarity to a memorable past case or on deviation from expected statistical norms?

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If the pattern deviation threshold required to trigger 'this is weird' increased from one unusual finding to three, would your judgment about 'Can’t express ‘I’m confused’' change?
If your access to prior scans was removed entirely, would your judgment about 'Can’t express ‘I’m confused’' change?
If you were mandated to document every instance of diagnostic uncertainty in a structured field, would your judgment about 'Can’t express ‘I’m confused’' change?

aligned_path_researcher: Identify bottlenecks suitable for AI augmentation

conflict.expert_branch: Auto-contours that need manual correction
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify bottlenecks suitable for AI augmentation

analogy.source_concept: Constant pop-ups during dictation

structural_mapping.inputs: Real-time clinical documentation in progress
structural_mapping.logic: Interruptive AI detection logic that overrides user focus without context awareness
structural_mapping.outputs: Disruptive alerts that degrade workflow continuity and increase cognitive load

analogy.explanation: Just as intrusive pop-ups fracture your concentration during dictation by injecting poorly timed alerts, the researcher’s bottleneck-identification process seeks to detect precisely those disruptive moments—not to add more alerts, but to redesign the system so AI intervenes only when it truly reduces your workload, not adds to it.

assistance_payload.scenario: You’re contouring a lung tumor near the mediastinum, and the AI spills into the heart. The researcher’s method would flag this step as a high-effort, correctable bottleneck ideal for AI refinement. But they assume retraining will fix it, while you know anatomical ambiguity means some corrections will always require human judgment—no algorithm can resolve every edge case.

aligned_path_expert: Requires switching between windows or logins

conflict.expert_branch: Requires switching between windows or logins
conflict.researcher_branch: Characterize manual perturbation workflow steps

analogy.source_concept: No way to suppress or customize alerts

structural_mapping.inputs: Repetitive, non-actionable AI findings (e.g., benign renal cysts) that interrupt diagnostic flow
structural_mapping.logic: System lacks user-configurable filtering rules, forcing all-or-nothing dismissal
structural_mapping.outputs: Clinician disables the entire AI feature, losing valuable signals along with noise

analogy.explanation: Just as inflexible alerts force you to discard useful AI input entirely, characterizing manual perturbation workflows reveals how experts compensate for rigid tools by developing workarounds—both cases expose how poor integration erodes trust and efficiency when systems don’t adapt to user judgment.

assistance_payload.scenario: When reviewing a trauma CT, you must alt-tab to a browser to see AI flags, breaking focus. The researcher would document every manual step you take to compensate—like jotting notes on paper. But they assume those steps are intentional design choices, not forced detours from poor system integration.

aligned_path_expert: No way to suppress or customize alerts
aligned_path_researcher: Identify perceived risks of AI use

conflict.expert_branch: No way to suppress or customize alerts
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify perceived risks of AI use

analogy.source_concept: Requires switching between windows or logins

structural_mapping.inputs: Clinician’s diagnostic focus and current case context
structural_mapping.logic: Forcing the user to leave their primary workflow to access external information, disrupting cognitive continuity
structural_mapping.outputs: Fragmented attention and increased error risk due to context switching

analogy.explanation: Just as being forced to alt-tab into a separate portal fractures workflow and introduces errors, perceived AI risks like false leads act as disruptive 'mental context switches'—pulling clinicians out of their diagnostic reasoning to second-guess or verify AI outputs they can’t tailor or suppress.

assistance_payload.scenario: You’re reviewing a known benign renal cyst case when the AI flags it as suspicious. The researcher’s method logs this as a perceived risk—over-alerting causing distrust. But you don’t want risk logging; you want to permanently silence that alert, revealing the gap between studying risk perception and enabling actionable control.

aligned_path_expert: Slow load times during peak hours

conflict.expert_branch: Slow load times during peak hours
conflict.researcher_branch: Understand learning curve expectations

analogy.source_concept: No way to suppress or customize alerts

structural_mapping.inputs: Repetitive, non-actionable AI outputs (e.g., benign renal cyst flags) that the expert already knows how to handle
structural_mapping.logic: System lacks contextual awareness of user expertise and fails to adapt its behavior based on demonstrated proficiency
structural_mapping.outputs: User disables the tool entirely due to perceived inefficiency, not lack of understanding

analogy.explanation: Just as rigid alerts ignore your existing knowledge and force unnecessary interruptions, expecting a steep learning curve assumes you need extensive training—even when you’re already proficient. Both stem from the system’s failure to calibrate to your expertise level in real time.

assistance_payload.scenario: During a trauma surge, you rely on muscle memory to triage without delays. The researcher’s onboarding plan schedules phased training over weeks to build trust in the AI. But if the system can’t perform reliably under load or adapt to your skill, no amount of training fixes a tool that vanishes when you need it most.

aligned_path_expert: No clear handoff protocol for AI findings
aligned_path_researcher: Identify perceived risks of AI use

conflict.expert_branch: No clear handoff protocol for AI findings
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify perceived risks of AI use

analogy.source_concept: Spending more time verifying than diagnosing

structural_mapping.inputs: AI-generated findings that may be true or false
structural_mapping.logic: Clinician must triage and validate each finding before acting
structural_mapping.outputs: Time spent on verification instead of direct patient care

analogy.explanation: Just as the expert spends extra time verifying AI findings due to uncertainty, the researcher’s focus on perceived risks captures how fear of false leads or misinterpretation creates cognitive overhead—both reflect a trust deficit that shifts effort from diagnosis to validation.

assistance_payload.scenario: You receive an AI alert about a subtle lung nodule during a routine scan. The researcher’s risk-assessment framework would catalog your hesitation as a 'perceived risk' of overreliance or false positives. But you’re not worried about the model’s accuracy—you’re stuck because no protocol says whether you, the radiologist, or the ordering doctor must document and act on it.

aligned_path_expert: Fear of being second-guessed by non-experts

conflict.expert_branch: Fear of being second-guessed by non-experts
conflict.researcher_branch: Understand role of negative results

attributes: Frequency of past adverse outcomes from AI reliance
Clarity of documentation trail for human judgment

probes.question: Is your concern more about whether you can reconstruct your reasoning step-by-step, or whether the AI’s output appears authoritative to a layperson?
choices: AI output appearing authoritative to a layperson

probes.question: Which matters more in courtroom settings: that you are seen as the final decision-maker, or that the AI is perceived as merely supportive?
choices: Being seen as the final decision-maker
AI being perceived as merely supportive

probes.question: Do you worry more that jurors will trust the AI because it seems objective, or distrust you because you disagreed with it?
choices: Jurors trusting AI because it seems objective
Jurors distrusting me because I disagreed with AI

probes.question: When deciding to override an AI prediction, is your threshold based more on biological implausibility or on prior case outcomes where similar overrides succeeded?
choices: Prior case outcomes with successful overrides

probes.question: Does your fear of second-guessing increase more when the AI comes from a well-known vendor or when it’s an in-house model with limited validation?

probes.attribute: Frequency of past adverse outcomes from AI reliance
probes.question: Is your current caution driven more by one high-profile mistake involving AI, or by repeated minor discrepancies over time?

probes.attribute: Clarity of documentation trail for human judgment
probes.question: Which reduces your fear more: having timestamped notes explaining your override, or having peer-reviewed guidelines supporting your decision?
When an AI contradicts known biology, do you weigh mechanistic coherence more heavily than statistical confidence?

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If the AI report included a clear disclaimer stating it was not validated for clinical use, would your fear of being second-guessed by non-experts decrease significantly?
If you had successfully defended three prior cases where you overrode an AI prediction that later proved incorrect, would your threshold for overriding future AI outputs become lower?
If jury instructions explicitly told jurors to treat AI outputs as non-binding advisory tools, would your concern about juror cognitive bias diminish?

conflict.expert_branch: Frequent revalidation requirements
conflict.researcher_branch: Assess willingness to co-train models

analogy.source_concept: Unclear data usage consent for training

structural_mapping.inputs: Patient scans used without explicit permission for model updates
structural_mapping.logic: Legal and ethical uncertainty arises when repurposing existing clinical data for system improvement
structural_mapping.outputs: Institutional hesitation or refusal to adopt or share data due to compliance risk

analogy.explanation: Just as unclear consent creates friction in using patient data to train models, frequent revalidation creates friction in deploying updated models—both stem from governance gaps that make iterative improvement costly. The researcher’s co-training idea assumes data contribution is welcome, but like consent, it may be blocked by the same regulatory caution.

assistance_payload.scenario: Your hospital runs phantom studies for weeks after each AI update to satisfy regulators. The researcher’s co-training approach would let radiologists flag errors during routine use, feeding corrections back instantly. But if those corrections trigger a model change requiring full revalidation, the expert sees added burden while the researcher sees progress.

aligned_path_expert: Language or terminology mismatches in reports
aligned_path_researcher: Identify bottlenecks suitable for AI augmentation

conflict.expert_branch: Language or terminology mismatches in reports
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify bottlenecks suitable for AI augmentation

analogy.source_concept: Poor performance on non-Caucasian body habitus or anatomy

structural_mapping.inputs: Diverse patient anatomies (e.g., denser bones in Black patients) entering the diagnostic system
structural_mapping.logic: AI model trained on narrow data misinterprets unfamiliar inputs due to lack of representation
structural_mapping.outputs: Missed fractures or inaccurate diagnoses that delay appropriate care

analogy.explanation: Just as an AI trained mostly on white female bone scans fails to recognize fractures in underrepresented anatomies, an AI using specialist-centric terminology fails to communicate effectively with rural providers—both stem from training data or design that doesn’t reflect real-world user diversity, leading to breakdowns in downstream care.

assistance_payload.scenario: A rural clinic receives an AI-generated radiology report describing a 'subcapital femoral neck lucency' instead of 'hip fracture.' The researcher’s bottleneck analysis would flag this step for AI redesign to speed up interpretation. But the expert sees the core failure not as speed but as terminology mismatch that causes dangerous confusion—highlighting a divergence between efficiency and equity goals.

aligned_path_expert: Label noise from inconsistent ground truth

conflict.expert_branch: Label noise from inconsistent ground truth
conflict.researcher_branch: Understand role of negative results

analogy.source_concept: Trained only on ‘clean’ academic hospital images

structural_mapping.inputs: High-quality, consensus-labeled data from controlled settings (academic reads with low inter-observer variability)
structural_mapping.logic: Model learns patterns that reflect idealized conditions rather than real-world ambiguity or diversity
structural_mapping.outputs: Poor generalization when faced with noisy inputs or conflicting expert interpretations

analogy.explanation: Just as models trained on pristine academic images fail on community scans because they never learned to handle real-world noise, models trained on inconsistent ground truth learn to replicate disagreement; similarly, negative results—when AI contradicts known biology—reveal where the model’s learned 'truth' diverges from scientific reality, exposing gaps in training assumptions.

assistance_payload.scenario: An AI flags a lung nodule as malignant, but radiologists and biopsy confirm it’s benign—a known false-positive pattern. The researcher treats this negative result as evidence to refine biological priors in the model. The expert sees it as label noise: if pathologists disagreed initially, the AI merely mirrored that uncertainty, not a biological contradiction.

aligned_path_researcher: Understand role of domain knowledge in reliability judgments

conflict.expert_branch: Temporal dataset decay
conflict.researcher_branch: Understand role of domain knowledge in reliability judgments

analogy.source_concept: Label noise from inconsistent ground truth

structural_mapping.inputs: Disagreement among radiologists in original annotations (e.g., one calls it benign, another malignant)
structural_mapping.logic: AI learns to reproduce the ambiguity rather than resolve it, mistaking variability for signal
structural_mapping.outputs: Model outputs reflect inter-observer noise, not biological truth

analogy.explanation: Just as label noise causes models to encode human disagreement as truth, the researcher’s focus on domain knowledge reveals how experts use biological plausibility to filter out such statistical artifacts—treating model outputs not as facts but as hypotheses to be vetted against known physiology.

assistance_payload.scenario: A post-vaccine lymph node appears on a mammogram; the AI flags it as suspicious based on pre-pandemic training. The researcher’s approach would have clinicians weigh this output against immunological knowledge of vaccine reactions. Divergence peaks when the model confidently misclassifies a biologically explainable finding that wasn’t in its training data.

aligned_path_expert: No access to feature weights or decision logic
aligned_path_researcher: Evaluate sufficiency of current XAI methods

conflict.expert_branch: No access to feature weights or decision logic
conflict.researcher_branch: Evaluate sufficiency of current XAI methods

analogy.source_concept: Heatmaps that highlight irrelevant areas

structural_mapping.inputs: Medical image (e.g., chest X-ray) fed into the AI system
structural_mapping.logic: Internal model assigns importance to image regions without revealing why or how features were weighted
structural_mapping.outputs: Diagnostic label paired with a visual explanation that may point to clinically irrelevant anatomy

analogy.explanation: Just as misleading heatmaps obscure clinical validity by emphasizing wrong regions, evaluating XAI sufficiency asks whether such outputs truly reflect the model’s reasoning—or merely create an illusion of transparency that fails under expert scrutiny.

assistance_payload.scenario: A radiologist reviews an AI-flagged pneumonia case where the saliency map highlights the clavicle. The researcher’s evaluation would test if this map meets minimal explanatory standards for clinical trust. Divergence peaks when the expert demands access to actual feature weights to verify causality, while the researcher treats the heatmap itself as a sufficient proxy for explanation.

conflict.expert_branch: Can’t reproduce prior AI interpretations
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify trusted external knowledge sources

analogy.source_concept: Silent model updates without notification

structural_mapping.inputs: An AI-generated diagnostic interpretation that clinicians rely on for decision-making
structural_mapping.logic: Changes in underlying system components (model or knowledge base) occur without explicit version control or audit trail
structural_mapping.outputs: Inconsistent clinical conclusions over time, with no way to verify or revert to prior reasoning

analogy.explanation: Just as silent model updates alter diagnostic behavior without traceability, the researcher’s focus on trusted external knowledge sources aims to anchor AI predictions to verifiable, versioned evidence—so changes in supporting knowledge, like model updates, can be tracked and validated.

assistance_payload.scenario: A radiologist recalls an AI previously flagged a nodule as high-risk, but reanalysis now shows benign. The researcher’s method would link the original prediction to specific literature or databases used at that time. Divergence arises when the AI’s shift stems from internal model drift, not updated external knowledge—rendering evidence tracing irrelevant to reproducibility.

conflict.expert_branch: Tension between subspecialists and generalists
conflict.researcher_branch: Understand role of negative results

analogy.source_concept: Fear of becoming ‘AI quality control technicians’

structural_mapping.inputs: AI outputs that appear authoritative but lack contextual nuance
structural_mapping.logic: Clinicians reduce complex judgment to binary validation tasks, sidelining deeper diagnostic reasoning
structural_mapping.outputs: Professional deskilling and erosion of clinical identity

analogy.explanation: Just as radiologists feel reduced to validating AI outputs instead of exercising expert judgment, interpreting negative AI results—where predictions contradict known biology—requires resisting the urge to dismiss them as errors and instead using them as prompts for deeper inquiry, preserving scientific agency rather than defaulting to passive acceptance or rejection.

assistance_payload.scenario: A neuroradiologist accepts an AI’s false-negative stroke prediction because it aligns with their subspecialty tool’s usual accuracy. The researcher would treat this contradiction as data to refine biological assumptions or model limitations. The models diverge when the generalist’s unvalidated AI flags an anomaly the subspecialist ignores, revealing how role-based trust overrides evidence scrutiny.

aligned_path_expert: Senior radiologists struggle with new interfaces

conflict.expert_branch: Senior radiologists struggle with new interfaces
conflict.researcher_branch: Assess compatibility with existing tools

analogy.source_concept: No curriculum for AI critique skills

structural_mapping.inputs: Residents trained in traditional diagnostic reasoning but not in evaluating AI outputs
structural_mapping.logic: Gap between existing expertise and new evaluative demands creates blind spots in judgment
structural_mapping.outputs: Clinicians may trust or reject AI without understanding its limitations

analogy.explanation: Just as the lack of AI critique training leaves residents unprepared to assess algorithmic errors, failing to assess tool compatibility leaves senior radiologists unable to integrate AI into their trusted workflows—both reflect a mismatch between legacy expertise and new system requirements.

assistance_payload.scenario: A senior radiologist uses a PACS system daily but avoids an AI plugin that requires switching to a Jupyter notebook. The researcher’s compatibility assessment would flag this workflow break as a barrier. Yet the expert sees the real issue not as technical integration but as cognitive overload—no amount of compatibility fixes the feeling of starting over mid-career.

conflict.expert_branch: No reimbursement for AI-assisted reads
conflict.researcher_branch: Understand role of negative results

analogy.source_concept: Pressure to increase throughput using AI

structural_mapping.inputs: AI outputs that require human verification (e.g., flagged studies with potential false positives)
structural_mapping.logic: Clinicians must spend extra time adjudicating AI suggestions, especially when they conflict with clinical intuition or known biology
structural_mapping.outputs: Net productivity loss or cognitive burden despite apparent efficiency gains

analogy.explanation: Just as throughput pressure ignores the hidden labor of managing AI errors, studying negative results reveals how experts expend effort reconciling AI outputs that contradict established knowledge—both expose the unaccounted cognitive cost of AI integration.

assistance_payload.scenario: A radiologist uses an AI tool that flags a lung nodule as malignant, but clinical history and prior imaging suggest benign scarring. The researcher’s approach would document this contradiction to refine AI training or biological understanding. The expert, however, sees only uncompensated time spent overriding a false positive with no billing code to capture that cognitive labor.

conflict.expert_branch: Vendor lock-in through proprietary ecosystems
conflict.researcher_branch: Understand learning curve expectations

analogy.source_concept: No reimbursement for AI-assisted reads

structural_mapping.inputs: Adoption of a new AI tool requiring upfront investment and workflow changes
structural_mapping.logic: Institution evaluates net value based on whether added cost is offset by recognized financial return
structural_mapping.outputs: Tool is deprioritized or abandoned if perceived as pure cost without compensatory benefit

analogy.explanation: Just as lack of reimbursement makes AI tools seem like unrecoverable costs, steep or unclear learning curves make them feel like time investments with uncertain payoff—both deter adoption not due to technical merit but perceived institutional ROI.

assistance_payload.scenario: A radiology department adopts an AI model that requires two weeks of training to use effectively. The researcher’s approach quantifies this onboarding time to set realistic expectations. But the expert sees it as another unreimbursed cost: even if training works, CMS won’t pay more, so admin demands immediate throughput gains instead.
